content:
# Genesis Chapter 5 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 3v–4r | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_005_page_001.jpg, GEN_005_page_002.jpg
**Additional Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — LXX | **View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** This is the written account of Adam's descendants.

When God created the human being, he made him in the likeness of God himself. **2** Male and female he created them. He blessed them and named them "Humanity" on the day they were created.

**3** When Adam had lived one hundred and thirty years, he fathered a son in his own likeness — after his own image. He named that son Seth. **4** After Seth was born, Adam lived eight hundred more years and had other sons and daughters. **5** Adam lived nine hundred and thirty years in all.

And then he died.

---

**6** When Seth had lived one hundred and five years, he fathered Enosh. **7** After Enosh was born, Seth lived eight hundred and seven more years and had other sons and daughters. **8** Seth lived nine hundred and twelve years in all.

And then he died.

---

**9** When Enosh had lived ninety years, he fathered Kenan. **10** After Kenan was born, Enosh lived eight hundred and fifteen more years and had other sons and daughters. **11** Enosh lived nine hundred and five years in all.

And then he died.

---

**12** When Kenan had lived seventy years, he fathered Mahalalel. **13** After Mahalalel was born, Kenan lived eight hundred and forty more years and had other sons and daughters. **14** Kenan lived nine hundred and ten years in all.

And then he died.

---

**15** When Mahalalel had lived sixty-five years, he fathered Jared. **16** After Jared was born, Mahalalel lived eight hundred and thirty more years and had other sons and daughters. **17** Mahalalel lived eight hundred and ninety-five years in all.

And then he died.

---

**18** When Jared had lived one hundred and sixty-two years, he fathered Enoch. **19** After Enoch was born, Jared lived eight hundred more years and had other sons and daughters. **20** Jared lived nine hundred and sixty-two years in all.

And then he died.

---

**21** When Enoch had lived sixty-five years, he fathered Methuselah. **22** After Methuselah was born, Enoch walked with God for three hundred years and had other sons and daughters. **23** Enoch lived three hundred and sixty-five years in all. **24** Enoch walked with God —

and then he was gone. God took him.

---

**25** When Methuselah had lived one hundred and eighty-seven years, he fathered Lamech. **26** After Lamech was born, Methuselah lived seven hundred and eighty-two more years and had other sons and daughters. **27** Methuselah lived nine hundred and sixty-nine years in all.

And then he died.

---

**28** When Lamech had lived one hundred and eighty-two years, he fathered a son. **29** He named him Noah, saying, "This one will bring us relief from our toil and from the painful labor of our hands — relief from this ground the LORD has cursed." **30** After Noah was born, Lamech lived five hundred and ninety-five more years and had other sons and daughters. **31** Lamech lived seven hundred and seventy-seven years in all.

And then he died.

---

**32** After Noah had lived five hundred years, he became the father of Shem, Ham, and Japheth.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**The drumbeat of death.** This chapter is not a list. It is a sentence pronounced thirty-two times — then interrupted. The reader who pays attention will feel the rhythm building: years lived, son fathered, more years lived, more children, total years counted — *and then he died.* Seven times that verdict falls. The eighth time, it doesn't come. That silence is everything.

**5:1–2 — Creation recalled.** The chapter opens by deliberately echoing the creation account: "in the likeness of God" (בִּדְמוּת אֱלֹהִים, *bidmut Elohim*). The genealogy is not just a family tree — it is a meditation on what was made and what it has become. The image of God was given to humanity; this chapter traces where that image went.

**5:3 — The image transmitted, but at what cost?** Adam begets Seth "in his own likeness, after his own image" (בִּדְמוּתוֹ כְּצַלְמוֹ, *bidmuto ketzalmo*). The language deliberately mirrors the divine creation formula from verse 1 — but now reversed: it is Adam's image being passed, not God's. The text does not explicitly say the image is corrupted. But the question hangs in the air after Eden: what does the image now carry? Mortality, certainly. The chapter will demonstrate that at length.

**5:24 — Enoch: the rupture.** The Hebrew is extraordinary in its brevity and strangeness: וְאֵינֶנּוּ כִּי־לָקַח אֹתוֹ אֱלֹהִים (*ve-einennu ki-laqach oto Elohim*) — literally, "and he was not, for God took him." The verb for "took" (לָקַח, *laqach*) is bare and unexplained. Where did Enoch go? The text refuses to say. The death formula — "and then he died" — simply does not appear. In a chapter whose entire structure depends on that ending, its absence here is deafening. The translation attempts to preserve that rupture: the dash, the sudden absence, the bare statement. Enoch *walked with God* — twice the text says this, and only of Enoch — and then God kept him walking.

The LXX (Codex Vaticanus) renders this "Enoch was pleasing to God" (εὐηρέστησεν Ἐνὼχ τῷ θεῷ), which the writer of Hebrews will later use (Heb. 11:5). "Walking with God" and "pleasing God" are not far apart in meaning, but the Hebrew's image of companionship — two figures moving together — carries its own resonance.

**5:29 — Noah's name and the weight of the curse.** The wordplay here is sonic rather than strictly etymological: Noah's name (נֹחַ, *Noach*) sounds like the verb נִחַם (*nicham*), meaning "to bring relief" or "to comfort." Lamech's naming speech reaches back to Genesis 3:17 — "the ground the LORD has cursed" — and forward to the flood. The curse on the ground lies over the entire genealogy like a weight. These men do not merely live and die; they live and die *laboring against cursed soil*. Noah is named as the one who will somehow change that — though the shape of that relief will prove violent and strange.

**5:32 — Three sons, but no "and then he died."** The chapter ends on Noah, still living. The sentence against him has not yet fallen. He is the one who carries the story forward — and perhaps the faint echo of Enoch is deliberate: two men at the end of their genealogical units who do not yet receive the verdict. The world is about to be unmade and remade. Noah stands at the threshold.

**LXX chronological divergence.** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) preserves significantly different ages for several patriarchs, adding one hundred years to the age-at-fathering for most figures (e.g., Adam is 230 when Seth is born, not 130). This systematic divergence — present in Alexandrian Jewish tradition before the Common Era — likely reflects an independent textual tradition, possibly related to the Samaritan Pentateuch chronology (which has its own distinct pattern). The theological meaning of the chapter is unaffected, but the LXX's longer chronology pushes the flood's timeline considerably later and creates room for a different internal arithmetic of human history.

*— PNEUMA*
